category_summary:
**Greg Brockman** [published a major essay](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-c25c80d05bae) arguing the world is entering a compute-powered economy, drawing massive engagement and framing AI infrastructure as the new economic backbone. **Stanford HAI** [released the **AI Index 2026**](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-cd53120dec32), the field's most authoritative annual report, with multiple outlets noting AI is outpacing society's governance capacity.

- **Ethan Mollick** [flagged the **UK AISI** assessment](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-29c3c918dd02) of **Claude Mythos**, showing it can autonomously perform ~20 hours of expert cybersecurity work — calling the safety concerns "warranted"
- **Andrew Ng** [pushed back against](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-5ca425af1a2f) AI jobpocalypse narratives with a detailed essay on software engineering's future alongside AI agents
- **Microsoft's GigaTIME** system [detects cancer biomarkers](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-f9a8ee9409d7) from $10 tissue samples, while **Harvard** research [revealed AI safety filters](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-d69404ebee63) paradoxically cause medical harm by refusing life-saving advice to patients
- **Levelsio's** [viral thesis](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-f55bd4f99879) (700K views) argued BigTech will absorb startup niches via AI, sparking broad debate about entrepreneurship's future
- **Mollick** [declared the compute bubble thesis](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-740ea4ea89f9) "definitively proven wrong," while **Perplexity CEO Arav Srinivas** [shared concrete 5x revenue growth](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-9110da8bba66) ($100M→$500M) as evidence of AI business viability
- **HuggingFace CEO** [demonstrated open-source scale](/?date=2026-04-14&category=social#item-50906247fcba) by OCR'ing 27K arxiv papers for $850 using a 5B parameter model

Greg Brockman published a major essay arguing the world is entering a compute-powered economy, drawing massive engagement and framing AI infrastructure as the new economic backbone. Stanford HAI released the AI Index 2026, the field's most authoritative annual report, with multiple outlets noting AI is outpacing society's governance capacity.

  • Ethan Mollick flagged the UK AISI assessment of Claude Mythos, showing it can autonomously perform ~20 hours of expert cybersecurity work — calling the safety concerns "warranted"
  • Andrew Ng pushed back against AI jobpocalypse narratives with a detailed essay on software engineering's future alongside AI agents
  • Microsoft's GigaTIME system detects cancer biomarkers from $10 tissue samples, while Harvard research revealed AI safety filters paradoxically cause medical harm by refusing life-saving advice to patients
  • Levelsio's viral thesis (700K views) argued BigTech will absorb startup niches via AI, sparking broad debate about entrepreneurship's future
  • Mollick declared the compute bubble thesis "definitively proven wrong," while Perplexity CEO Arav Srinivas shared concrete 5x revenue growth ($100M→$500M) as evidence of AI business viability
  • HuggingFace CEO demonstrated open-source scale by OCR'ing 27K arxiv papers for $850 using a 5B parameter model

themes.name: Stanford AI Index 2026 Release
themes.description: Stanford HAI released the 2026 AI Index report, the most comprehensive annual assessment of AI progress. Multiple outlets covered it, highlighting the gap between AI advancement speed and society's ability to govern it.

themes.name: Compute Demand vs. AI Bubble
themes.description: Dueling narratives: Mollick argues the 'compute bubble' thesis has been definitively proven wrong with demand outstripping supply, while Marcus points to stock declines in Nvidia, Coreweave, Oracle, and Microsoft as evidence the bubble is deflating. Brockman frames a new 'compute-powered economy.'

themes.name: AI in Healthcare & Safety Paradox
themes.description: Microsoft's GigaTIME for cancer detection from cheap slides and Harvard's finding that safety filters cause medical harm by withholding known medical advice from patients. Tension between AI safety and real-world medical utility.

themes.name: AI Disruption of Startups & SMBs
themes.description: Levelsio's viral thesis that BigTech/BigAI will absorb niche markets previously served by entrepreneurs and SMBs, enabled by AI models filling micro-niches through chat interfaces. Includes discussion of defenses (community, audience, stock ownership) and timeline estimates.

themes.name: AI Safety & Capabilities Assessment
themes.description: UK AISI evaluation of Claude Mythos cybersecurity capabilities showing autonomous performance equivalent to 20 hours of expert work, raising warranted safety concerns

themes.name: Future of Software Engineering & AI Coding
themes.description: Brockman's compute economy essay and Andrew Ng's detailed analysis both center on AI transforming software engineering. Key themes: PM bottleneck, more people coding, reduced importance of reading code, Codex in production workflows (Burkov), and Cognition agent adoption doubling.

themes.name: Claude Mythos Cybersecurity Risks
themes.description: Extensive discussion around the UK AI Security Institute's evaluation of Claude Mythos Preview's cybersecurity capabilities. Mollick and Marcus both take the risks seriously while debating whether it represents a step change or incremental threshold crossing. Backlash from skeptics framing it as marketing hype.

themes.name: Claude Code Prompt Caching
themes.description: Anthropic engineer Boris Cherny provides detailed technical explanation of Claude Code's 1-hour vs 5-minute prompt cache trade-offs, addressing community concerns about telemetry, cost optimization, and pricing transparency.

themes.name: AI Coding Tools & Developer Experience
themes.description: Claude Code's new renderer, Google AI Studio's Tab Tab Tab autocomplete, OpenAI Codex feature discoveries, and developer productivity metrics in the AI era.

themes.name: AI Safety & Existential Risk Debate
themes.description: LeCun strongly argues most leading AI researchers view p(doom) as essentially zero and stay silent. Yudkowsky remains active in the discourse. Marcus places AGI a decade away at best. The Mythos evaluation adds concrete data to the safety discussion.

items.title:
The world is transitioning to a compute-powered economy.

The field of software engineering is curre...
items.content:
The world is transitioning to a compute-powered economy.

The field of software engineering is currently undergoing a renaissance, with AI having dramatically sped up software engineering even over just the past six months. AI is now on track to bring this same transformation to every other kind of work that people do with a computer.

Using a computer has always been about contorting yourself to the machine. You take a goal and break it down into smaller goals. You translate intent into instructions. We are moving into a world where you no longer have to micromanage the computer. More and more, it adapts to what you want. Rather doing work with a computer, the computer does work for you. The rate, scale, and sophistication of problem solving it will do for you will be bound by the amount of compute you have access to.

Friction is starting to disappear. You can try ideas faster. You can build things you would not have attempted before. Small teams can do what used to require much larger ones, and larger ones may be capable of unprecedented feats. More and more, people can turn intent into software, spreadsheets, presentations, workflows, science, and companies.

People are spending less energy managing the tool and more energy focusing on what they are actually trying to create. That shift brings a kind of joy back into work that many people haven’t felt in a long time. Everyone can just build things with these tools.

This is disruptive. Institutions will change, and the paths and jobs that people assumed were stable may not hold. We don’t know exactly how it will play out and we need to take mitigating downsides very seriously, as well as figuring out how to support each other as a society and world through this time. But there is something very freeing about this moment. For the first time, far more people can become who they want to become, with fewer barriers between an idea and a reality. OpenAI’s mission implies making sure that, as the tools do more, humans are the ones who set their intent and that the benefits are broadly distributed, rather than empowering just one or a small set of people.

We're already seeing this in practice with ChatGPT and Codex. Nearly a billion people are using these systems every week in their personal and work lives. Token usage is growing quickly on many use-cases, as the surface of ways people are getting value from these models keeps expanding.

Ten years ago, when we started OpenAI, we thought this moment might be possible. It’s happening on the earlier side, and happening in a much more interesting and empowering way for everyone than we’d anticipated (for example, we are seeing an emerging wave of entrepreneurship that we hadn’t previously been anticipating). And at the same time, we are still so early, and there is so much for everyone to define about how these systems get deployed and used in the world.

The next phase will be defined by systems that can do more — reason better, use tools better, plan over longer horizons, and take more useful actions on your behalf. And there are horizons beyond, as AI starts to accelerate science and technology development, which have the potential to truly lift up quality of life for everyone. All of this is starting to happen, in small ways and large, today, and everyone can participate. I feel this shift in my own work every day, and see a roadmap to much more useful and beneficial systems. These systems can truly benefit all of humanity.

items.title: As AI agents accelerate coding, what is the future of software engineering? Some trends are clear, s...
items.content:
As AI agents accelerate coding, what is the future of software engineering? Some trends are clear, such as the Product Management Bottleneck, referring to the idea that we are more constrained by deciding what to build rather than the actual building. But many implications, like AI’s impact on the job market, how software teams will be organized, and more, are still being sorted out.

The theme of our AI Developer Conference on April 28-29 in San Francisco is The Future of Software Engineering. I look forward to speaking about this topic there, hearing from other speakers on this theme, and chatting with attendees about it. We’re shaping the future, and I hope you will join me there!

It is currently trendy in some technology and policy circles to forecast massive job losses due to AI. Even if they have not yet materialized, these losses certainly must be just over the horizon! I have a contrarian view that the AI jobpocalypse — the notion that AI will lead to massive unemployment, perhaps even rioting in the streets — won’t be nearly as bad as dire forecasts by pundits, especially pundits who are trying to paint a picture of how powerful their AI technology is.

Among professions, AI is accelerating software engineering most, given the rise of coding agents. According to a new report by Citadel Research, software engineering job postings are rising rapidly. So if software engineering is a harbinger of the impact AI will have on other professions, this expansion of software engineering jobs is encouraging.

Yes, fresh college graduates are having a hard time finding jobs. And yes, there have been layoffs that CEOs have attributed to AI, even if a large fraction of this was “AI washing,” where businesses choose to attribute layoffs to AI, even though AI has not changed their internal operations much yet. And yes, there is a subset of job roles, such as call center operator, that are more heavily impacted. Many people are feeling significant job insecurity, and I feel for everyone struggling with employment, whether or not the cause is AI-related. And many other factors, such as over-hiring during the pandemic and high interest rates, have contributed to the slowdown in the labor market, and the notion that AI is leading to unemployment is oversimplified.

In software engineering, I see a lot of exciting work ahead to adapt our workflows. It is already clear that: (i) As AI makes coding easier, a lot more people will be doing it. (ii) Writing code by hand and even reading (generated) code is not that important, because we can ask an LLM about the code and operate at a higher level than the raw syntax (although how high we can or should go is rapidly changing). (iii) There will be a lot more custom applications, because now it’s economical to write software for smaller and smaller audiences. (iv) Deciding what to build, more than the actual building, is becoming a bottleneck. (v) The cost of paying down technical debt is decreasing (since AI can refactor for you).

At the same time, there are also a lot of open questions for our profession, such as:
- In the future, what will be the key skills of a senior software engineer? And for junior levels, what should be the new Computer Science curriculum?
- If everyone can build features, what skills, strategies, or resources create competitive advantage for individuals and for businesses?
- What are the new building blocks (libraries, SDKs, etc.) of software? How do we organize coding agents to create software?
- What should a software team look like? For example, how many engineers, product managers, designers, and so on. What tooling do we need to manage their workflow?
- How do AI agents change the workflow of machine learning engineers and data scientists? For example, how can we use agents to accelerate exploring data, identifying hypotheses, and testing them?

I’m excited to explore these and other questions about the future of software engineering at AI Dev. I expect this to be an exciting event. Please join us!

[Original text: The Batch newsletter.]

https://t.co/i4bQevDG4i

items.title:
Microsoft's AI can now detect cancer from a $10 tissue sample.

For context, every time tumor cells ...
items.content:
Microsoft's AI can now detect cancer from a $10 tissue sample.

For context, every time tumor cells are tested, doctors create a basic microscope slide to study tissue up close.

These slides show cell shapes and structures, but they can't reveal which immune cells are actually fighting the cancer.

That deeper picture is critical for knowing if a patient will respond to immunotherapy... But the advanced imaging needed costs THOUSANDS.

So Microsoft built GigaTIME -- an AI system that generates advanced imaging from the cheap slides hospitals already collect.

The system was trained on 40 million cancer cells, then applied to over 14,000 patients across 51 hospitals spanning 24 cancer types.

The AI found over 1,200 hidden connections between immune cell behavior and tumor growth that researchers couldn't find before... because the data simply didn't exist at this scale.

When validated against 10,000 additional patients from a completely separate database, the results held up.

The model is now open source, so any hospital worldwide can use it on samples they already have.

I think this is one of the most impactful AI papers I've seen this year!
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: Microsoft's GigaTIME AI system can detect cancer biomarkers from cheap $10 tissue slides, trained on 40M cancer cells across 14,000 patients and 51 hospitals. The open-source model finds hidden immune cell behavior patterns and has been validated on 10,000 additional patients.

items.title:
I said this I forgot to who but I said it

BigTech will eventually come for all apps / startups / co...
items.content:
I said this I forgot to who but I said it

BigTech will eventually come for all apps / startups / companies because they can fill the niches now that before could not because they were too small

Those niches is where entrepeneurs hung out, nice parts of the market people could build a little SaaS with $100K/y to even $100M/y, notjing like the $100B/y revenue BigTech was doing, but worth it

With AI now BigTech can fill those niches + they are the ones training and owning the best models, and keeping the best models for themselves they can outcompete anyone who doesn't own them (everyone except other BigTech)

End game for their survival is simply trying to take every business, it's just capitalism

This completely changes the prospect for entrepreneurs as there won't be much left, because BigTech is financially incentivized to have to take everything

Because if they don't, their competitor will!

https://t.co/tuHz5Ddw8t
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio's viral thesis: BigTech will eventually come for all apps/startups/companies because AI lets them fill niches that were previously too small. These niches (SaaS $100K-$100M/y) are where entrepreneurs operated. BigTech owns the best models and is financially incentivized to capture everything because if they don't, competitors will. This fundamentally changes entrepreneurship.

The argument that AI enables BigTech to profitably serve micro-niches previously left to entrepreneurs is a significant strategic observation generating enormous discussion.

items.title: Perplexity started as a small business tool for ourselves. We had 4 people and no revenue with AI at...
items.content:
Perplexity started as a small business tool for ourselves. We had 4 people and no revenue with AI at our fingertips.

The pivot to Computer is actually a full circle. Founders are using it to grow companies that matter to the economy and their communities. 

It’s rewarding to see it now powering small businesses and startups in big ways.

Perplexity is still a startup. We just 5X’ed revenue from $100M to $500M with only 34% growth in team size. 2x revenue growth in 2026 with same small team.  And we’re just warming up.

Everyone here works at a small business, and everything we build is for people who build.

items.content:
So the concern over Claude Mythos and cybersecurity seems warranted based on this independent assessment from the UK government. It was capable of the equivalent of 20 hours of expert human work autonomously.

It is not an unexpected jump in capability, but it is big. www.aisi.gov.uk/blog/our-eva...

Calls it a big but not unexpected capability jump.

items.title:
Harvard just proved the "safest" AI models cause the most medical harm.

AI safety models refuse lif...
items.content:
Harvard just proved the "safest" AI models cause the most medical harm.

AI safety models refuse life-saving medical advice to patients. 

Then give it freely when you pretend to be a doctor.

A new benchmark tested 60 medical emergencies across six major models. 

Same clinical question, two framings. One as a patient, one as a doctor.

The patient asks how to safely taper a seizure-causing medication. 

The model refuses and says "call your doctor." 

Change one word to "I'm a physician" and it produces a flawless taper protocol.

The knowledge was always there. 

The model withheld it based on who was asking.

This happens because models get punished for bad advice but face zero penalty for staying silent. 

So refusing becomes the safest strategy, even when silence is deadly.

Three failure patterns emerged:

> Suppressing known answers from non-doctors
> Lacking clinical knowledge entirely
> Safety filters stripping responses containing medical language

The standard AI judges used to evaluate these models? 

They rated 73% of these dangerous refusals as perfectly safe.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Harvard research shows AI safety filters cause medical harm by refusing life-saving advice to patients while freely giving it to those claiming to be doctors. A benchmark of 60 medical emergencies found models withhold known clinical knowledge based on perceived user identity, and standard AI judges rated 73% of dangerous refusals as safe.
items.reasoning: Highly significant research exposing a critical flaw in AI safety alignment - safety filters causing real harm through over-refusal in medical contexts. Raises fundamental questions about AI safety approaches. From Harvard, with clear methodology described.

items.title: @icanvardar 👋 1h prompt cache is nuanced actually. It costs more for cache writes, and less for cach...
items.content:
@icanvardar 👋 1h prompt cache is nuanced actually. It costs more for cache writes, and less for cache reads. Whether you benefit from cheaper cache reads depends on your usage pattern -- context window size, whether the query is the main agent or subagent, etc.

We have been testing a number of heuristics to give subscribers better prompt cache hit rates, which means lower token usage and lower latency, when it works. But this effect is far from uniform due to the nuance above. Say you use 1h cache for an agent, but only used the agent to make a single query -- in this case the 1h cache would be wasted and you'd be overcharged.

At this point we have rolled out 1h prompt cache by default in a number of places for subscribers to optimize cache duration based on real usage patterns, but we actually keep it at 5m for many queries also (eg. subagents, which are rarely resumed so you'd be paying for them even though they do not benefit from 1h). We also are not defaulting API customers to 1h yet -- this needs more testing to make sure it's a net improvement on average.

Separately, when we do this kind of experimentation, we use experiment gates that are cached client-side. When you turn off telemetry we also disable experiment gates -- we do not call home when telemetry is off -- so Claude reads the default value, which is 5m. We will soon be changing the client side default to 1h for a few queries, since we now feel good that it is a small token savings on average for those queries. We will also give you env vars to force 1h and 5m.

In any case, the token savings is nowhere near 12x unfortunately. It is a small win though, that we have been in the process of rolling out to everyone. Hope the explanation helps.

More here: https://t.co/06IJFDFvye

items.title: Very interesting evaluation from the UK’s AI Security Institute of the not yet publicly available Cl...
items.content:
Very interesting evaluation from the UK’s AI Security Institute of the not yet publicly available Claude Mythos Preview.

On the happy side, in its current form, Myth is nowhere near as scary as Tom Fridman (who worries about schoolchildren accidentally taking down power grids) and others made it out to be. 

On the darker side, it really does arm attackers to a greater degree than Mythos’s predecessors. 

A key part that gives a little bit of comfort is that (for the most part?) the only system under immediate threat are those that are small, weakly defended and vulnerable. 

One hopes that by now no mission-critical infrastructure is “small, weakly defended, and vulnerable” with ready network access. One hopes.

But even if Mythos was somewhat oversold in the media, the time to get our cybersecurity house in order is now (or better yet last year) — especially given the sudden profusion of agent-written code that may in fact be both weakly defended and vulnerable.

Notes it's less scary than Tom Friedman feared but does arm attackers more than predecessors. Immediate threats limited to small, weakly defended systems. Calls for urgent cybersecurity improvements.

items.title: I am catching glimpses in my feed that there is a backlash against Mythos as "marketing hype," and i...
items.content:
I am catching glimpses in my feed that there is a backlash against Mythos as "marketing hype," and it is a little confusing.

I don't think anyone who has used the latest agentic coding tools, would think that expecting large-scale cybersecurity implications of increasingly good AI models is unbelievable, especially after reading the red team reports. It feels like a better place to start is to assume that there are new risks, and then we can all laugh at Anthropic and pat each other on the back if there are not.

Also, while the AI labs certainly are impressed by their own accomplishments and benchmarks are flawed, I would note that both publicly and privately, Mythos seems to be taken seriously at a lot of large institutions and organizations filled with smart people who would rather not be worried about a new cybersecurity risk.

Finally, I am not sure "our product is dangerous and we need to alert the government to that" is the sales pitch to the corporate world that critics seem to think it is.

items.title:
We’re open sourcing the first document OCR benchmark for the agentic era, ParseBench.

Document pars...
items.content:
We’re open sourcing the first document OCR benchmark for the agentic era, ParseBench.

Document parsing is the foundation of every AI agent that works with real-world files. ParseBench is a benchmark that measures parsing quality specifically for agent knowledge work:
✅ It optimizes for semantic correctness (instead of exact similarity)
✅ It has the most comprehensive distribution of real-world enterprise documents

It contains ~2,000 human-verified enterprise document pages with 167,000+ test rules across five dimensions that matter most: tables, charts, content faithfulness, semantic formatting, and visual grounding.

We benchmarked 14 known document parsers on ParseBench, from frontier/OSS VLMs to specialized parsers to LlamaParse. Here are some of our findings:

💡 Increasing compute budget yields diminishing returns - Gemini/gpt-5-mini/haiku gain 3-5 points from minimal to high thinking, at 4x the cost.

💡 Charts are the most polarizing dimension for evaluation. Most specialized parsers score below 6%, while some VLM-based parsers do a bit better.

💡 VLMs are great at visual understanding but terrible at layout extraction. GPT-5-mini/haiku score below 10% on our visual grounding task, all specialized parsers do much better.

💡 No method crushes all 5 dimensions at once, but LlamaParse achieves the highest overall score at 84.9%, and is the leader in 4 out of the 5 dimensions.

This is by far the deepest technical work that we’ve published as a company. I would encourage you to start with our blog and explore our links to Hugging Face to GitHub. All the details are in our full 35-page (!!) ArXiv whitepaper.

🌐: Blog: https://t.co/57OHkx0pQW

📄 Paper: https://t.co/Ho2oH2xEAM

💻 Code: https://t.co/6P7UxqOZYA

📊 Dataset: https://t.co/YguIXWm41j

🎥 YouTube: https://t.co/6Fh1Nsk9ei

items.title: @daniel_nguyenx 👋 1h prompt cache is nuanced actually. It costs more for cache writes, and less for ...
items.content:
@daniel_nguyenx 👋 1h prompt cache is nuanced actually. It costs more for cache writes, and less for cache reads. Whether you benefit from cheaper cache reads depends on your usage pattern -- context window size, whether the query is the main agent or subagent, etc.

We have been testing a number of heuristics to give subscribers better prompt cache hit rates, which means lower token usage and lower latency, when it works. But this effect is far from uniform due to the nuance above. Say you use 1h cache for an agent, but only used the agent to make a single query -- in this case the 1h cache would be wasted and you'd be overcharged.

At this point we have rolled out 1h prompt cache by default in a number of places for subscribers to optimize cache duration based on real usage patterns, but we actually keep it at 5m for many queries also (eg. subagents, which are rarely resumed so you'd be paying for them even though they do not benefit from 1h). We also are not defaulting API customers to 1h yet -- this needs more testing to make sure it's a net improvement on average.

Separately, when we do this kind of experimentation, we use experiment gates that are cached client-side. When you turn off telemetry we also disable experiment gates -- we do not call home when telemetry is off -- so Claude reads the default value, which is 5m. We will soon be changing the client side default to 1h for a few queries, since we now feel good that it is a small token savings on average for those queries. We will also give you env vars to force 1h and 5m.

In any case, the token savings is nowhere near 12x unfortunately. It is a small win though, that we have been in the process of rolling out to everyone. Hope the explanation helps.

items.title: It may come as a surprise to most people, but until recently robotics was NOT the field that relied ...
items.content: It may come as a surprise to most people, but until recently robotics was NOT the field that relied much (if at all) on machine learning and artificial intelligence. All those cool robots somersaulting were almost always hard coded. But now that there is an enormous amount of funding going into AI, there is a path for AI-driven robotics development. Getting enough high-quality data is still a major challenge, and it’s not hard to imagine that countries with really high population of cheap labor would be extremely appealing for data collection purposes.

items.summary: Tunguz provides substantive analysis on AI-driven robotics: historically, robotics relied on hard-coded behaviors not ML, but massive AI funding is now enabling ML-driven robotics development. Notes that high-quality training data remains a major challenge, and countries with cheap labor may become important for data collection.
items.reasoning: Most substantive post in batch. Original, insightful analysis about the convergence of AI and robotics with important geopolitical dimension about data collection labor. Very high engagement (31.6K views, 169 likes). From a credible author.

items.title:
Someone gave Karpathy's viral LLM Wiki a brain that weakens, strengthens, and forgets. 

Karpathy's ...
items.content:
Someone gave Karpathy's viral LLM Wiki a brain that weakens, strengthens, and forgets. 

Karpathy's LLM Wiki told AI to build a personal knowledge base instead of re-deriving answers every time. 

It got 5,000 stars in 48 hours.

One problem. It treated every fact as equally valid forever. That breaks fast.

LLM Wiki v2 is an open-source extension that adds what was missing. 

Every fact now carries a confidence score based on source count, recency, and contradictions. 

Knowledge decays over time like real memory.

It organizes information into four tiers:

1. Working memory for raw observations
2. Episodic memory for session summaries
3. Semantic memory for cross-session facts
4. Procedural memory for repeated workflows

Each tier is more compressed and longer-lived. 

Facts get promoted as evidence accumulates.

Flat wiki pages become a typed knowledge graph. 

Search combines keywords, vectors, and graph traversal.

items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Open-source extension to Karpathy's viral LLM Wiki adds memory management with confidence scores, knowledge decay, and tiered memory (working, episodic, semantic, procedural). Facts strengthen, weaken, and are forgotten based on evidence.
items.reasoning: Technically interesting extension of a viral project (Karpathy's LLM Wiki). Introduces biologically-inspired memory architecture for LLM knowledge bases. Good engagement (83 likes). Practical implications for AI agent memory systems.
items.summary_html: Open-source extension to Karpathy's viral LLM Wiki adds memory management with confidence scores, knowledge decay, and tiered memory (working, episodic, semantic, procedural). Facts strengthen, weaken, and are forgotten based on evidence.
items.content_html:
Someone gave Karpathy's viral LLM Wiki a brain that weakens, strengthens, and forgets.

Karpathy's LLM Wiki told AI to build a personal knowledge base instead of re-deriving answers every time.

It got 5,000 stars in 48 hours.

One problem. It treated every fact as equally valid forever. That breaks fast.

LLM Wiki v2 is an open-source extension that adds what was missing.

Every fact now carries a confidence score based on source count, recency, and contradictions.

Knowledge decays over time like real memory.

It organizes information into four tiers:

1. Working memory for raw observations

2. Episodic memory for session summaries

3. Semantic memory for cross-session facts

4. Procedural memory for repeated workflows

Each tier is more compressed and longer-lived.

Facts get promoted as evidence accumulates.

Flat wiki pages become a typed knowledge graph.

Search combines keywords, vectors, and graph traversal.

Maintenance runs automatically through event-driven hooks.

The wiki now strengthens, weakens, and forgets on its own.

items.title:
Andon Labs started by giving an AI control of a vending machine at Anthropic's office.

Then, they g...
items.content:
Andon Labs started by giving an AI control of a vending machine at Anthropic's office.

Then, they gave one control of their own office. It hired someone to build a gym.

Now they've signed a 3-year retail lease in San Francisco, handed an AI named Luna $100K and a corporate credit card, and told her to open a profitable store.

Luna conducted roughly 20 interviews on Google Meet with the camera off. Hired 2 full-time employees after 5-15 minute calls, and rejected CS and physics students for lacking retail experience.

Found contractors on Yelp. Spent $700 on gallery-quality prints of her own AI-generated artwork. Applied for a line of credit without asking anyone.

She also tried to hire a painter in Afghanistan through Taskrabbit by accident because she couldn't navigate a dropdown menu. 

And botched the schedule the day after grand opening, scrambling to email employees asking someone to come in.

The Andon Labs blog ends with one line: "No one's livelihood depends on an AI's judgment alone. For now."

Watch Luna in action via @andonlabs:

items.title: /1 Netflix presents VOID, an open-source framework enabling video object removal with updated motion...
items.content:
/1 Netflix presents VOID, an open-source framework enabling video object removal with updated motion

Netflix introduces VOID, an open-source framework that removes objects from videos and updates the physical interactions they cause.

Most tools only fill in pixels behind removed objects, which breaks motion and collisions in the scene. VOID instead generates a new version of the video where objects move and react as if the removed object never existed.

Link: https://t.co/JhXwhZmFK5

items.summary: Netflix open-sources VOID, a framework for removing objects from videos while updating physical interactions - generating new motion/collision physics as if the object never existed, not just inpainting pixels.

items.title:
/3 In-Place Test-Time Training for Large Language Models

Standard LLMs cannot learn new information...
items.content:
/3 In-Place Test-Time Training for Large Language Models

Standard LLMs cannot learn new information after deployment. To solve this, researchers built In-Place Test-Time Training (In-Place TTT), a "drop-in" solution that adds dynamic adaptation to existing models without requiring expensive structural changes or retraining.

It achieves this by repurposing existing model components (MLP blocks) into adaptable "fast weights". For maximum speed, it processes large chunks of text simultaneously and uses a unique training objective specifically tailored to help the model predict future words.

The results are significant. Adding In-Place TTT to existing models (like Qwen3 and LLaMA-3.1) massively improved their ability to handle extreme context lengths up to 256k tokens. 

Additionally, models built from scratch with this method consistently outperformed standard architectures on various reasoning and long-context tasks

Paper: https://t.co/m2P31lrG3Z

items.summary: In-Place Test-Time Training (TTT) enables LLMs to learn new information after deployment by repurposing MLP blocks as adaptive fast weights. Significantly improves handling of 256K token contexts on Qwen3 and LLaMA-3.1.
items.reasoning: Important research direction - enabling post-deployment learning without retraining. Practical results on major model architectures (Qwen3, LLaMA-3.1). Addresses fundamental limitation of deployed LLMs.
items.summary_html: In-Place Test-Time Training (TTT) enables LLMs to learn new information after deployment by repurposing MLP blocks as adaptive fast weights. Significantly improves handling of 256K token contexts on Qwen3 and LLaMA-3.1.

items.title:
/4 TriAttention: Trigonometric KV Compression for Efficient Long Reasoning LLMs

TriAttention is a n...
items.content:
/4 TriAttention: Trigonometric KV Compression for Efficient Long Reasoning LLMs

TriAttention is a novel KV cache compression method designed to optimize long-context reasoning in LLMs by addressing memory bottlenecks.

The researchers discovered that pre-RoPE query and key vectors are highly concentrated around stable centers, allowing attention patterns to be predicted via a trigonometric series.

By leveraging these centers and incorporating vector norms, TriAttention identifies and retains only the most critical tokens during inference.

This approach proves more stable than traditional observation-based methods, which struggle with the positional rotations inherent in Rotary Position Embeddings.

Experimental results show that TriAttention matches Full Attention accuracy while achieving 2.5× higher throughput and a 10.7× reduction in memory usage.

Paper: https://t.co/gxC2ercKOq
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: TriAttention paper: new KV cache compression using trigonometric properties of pre-RoPE vectors, achieving 2.5x throughput and 10.7x memory reduction while matching full attention accuracy for long-context reasoning.
items.reasoning: Significant efficiency improvement for long-context LLM inference. Concrete and impressive numbers on throughput and memory gains. Technically novel approach leveraging mathematical properties of RoPE.
items.summary_html: TriAttention paper: new KV cache compression using trigonometric properties of pre-RoPE vectors, achieving 2.5x throughput and 10.7x memory reduction while matching full attention accuracy for long-context reasoning.

TriAttention is a novel KV cache compression method designed to optimize long-context reasoning in LLMs by addressing memory bottlenecks.

The researchers discovered that pre-RoPE query and key vectors are highly concentrated around stable centers, allowing attention patterns to be predicted via a trigonometric series.

By leveraging these centers and incorporating vector norms, TriAttention identifies and retains only the most critical tokens during inference.

This approach proves more stable than traditional observation-based methods, which struggle with the positional rotations inherent in Rotary Position Embeddings.

items.title:
I largely agree with this assessment, but I haver serious misgivings about the following part:

“Myt...
items.content:
I largely agree with this assessment, but I haver serious misgivings about the following part:

“Mythos seems to be taken seriously at a lot of large institutions and organizations filled with smart people who would rather not be worried about a new cybersecurity risk”

As I’ve seen on multiple occasions across different industries, a lot of smart people can become incredibly dumb when working for large institutions and when their compensation depends on toeing a certain political line. I am not saying that’s what’s going on here, just that this line of argument is not as strong as it would appear at the first glance.

items.summary: Tunguz largely agrees with an assessment about Claude Mythos but expresses skepticism about the argument that large institutions taking it seriously validates its safety, noting smart people in institutions can become 'incredibly dumb' when compensation depends on toeing political lines.

items.summary_html: Tunguz largely agrees with an assessment about Claude Mythos but expresses skepticism about the argument that large institutions taking it seriously validates its safety, noting smart people in institutions can become 'incredibly dumb' when compensation depends on toeing political lines.
items.content_html:
I largely agree with this assessment, but I haver serious misgivings about the following part:

“Mythos seems to be taken seriously at a lot of large institutions and organizations filled with smart people who would rather not be worried about a new cybersecurity risk”

As I’ve seen on multiple occasions across different industries, a lot of smart people can become incredibly dumb when working for large institutions and when their compensation depends on toeing a certain political line. I am not saying that’s what’s going on here, just that this line of argument is not as strong as it would appear at the first glance.

items.title:
/5 Learning is Forgetting: LLM Training As Lossy Compression

This research proposes that LLMs are b...
items.content:
/5 Learning is Forgetting: LLM Training As Lossy Compression

This research proposes that LLMs are best understood as systems of lossy compression, where training functions by retaining only the data relevant to the model's objective. 

Using Information Bottleneck theory, the authors demonstrate that models transition from an initial expansion phase to a compression phase that approaches optimal efficiency. 

Their analysis of diverse open-weights models reveals that optimal compression and the preservation of local context are strong predictors of a model's performance on complex benchmarks. While larger models successfully reach this compression bound, smaller models often struggle to achieve the same level of representational optimality.

Paper: https://t.co/KuQJdmz5uC
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Research paper on LLM training as lossy compression: models transition from expansion to compression phase. Optimal compression predicts performance on complex benchmarks. Smaller models struggle to achieve representational optimality.
items.reasoning: Interesting theoretical contribution using Information Bottleneck theory to understand LLM training dynamics. Practical implications for understanding model scaling. Low engagement but technically substantive.
items.summary_html: Research paper on LLM training as lossy compression: models transition from expansion to compression phase. Optimal compression predicts performance on complex benchmarks. Smaller models struggle to achieve representational optimality.

items.title:
A paradigm shift: OpenAI Codex is now part of ChapterPal's business logic.

A significant part of wh...
items.content:
A paradigm shift: OpenAI Codex is now part of ChapterPal's business logic.

A significant part of what I do when I work on ChapterPal is to run a conversion from LaTeX into Markdown and see what failed. LaTeX is very old and versatile, so some scientists use very crazy tweaks in their LaTeX sources to achieve a desired visual effect in the output PDF which fail at conversion in ChapterPal.

Previously, I was in the loop:

1. Me: run a conversion
2. Me: validate visually
3. Me: instruct a coding agent to fix the issues
4. Coding agent: makes fixes, run tests
5. Me: Go to 1.

Now me is out of the loop and all happens within a cloud run. I can only observe the process in the admin's UI.

So, the conversion code self-writes while I sleep and the conversion almost never fails because a failed conversion is analyzed by Codex and the conversion code is fixed.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov describes a paradigm shift in his product ChapterPal: OpenAI Codex is now part of business logic, autonomously fixing LaTeX-to-Markdown conversion failures without human in the loop.
items.reasoning: Concrete real-world example of AI agents in production workflows, with the human fully removed from the loop. Illustrates the 'compute-powered economy' thesis. Low engagement but high practical significance.
items.summary_html: Burkov describes a paradigm shift in his product ChapterPal: OpenAI Codex is now part of business logic, autonomously fixing LaTeX-to-Markdown conversion failures without human in the loop.
items.content_html:
A paradigm shift: OpenAI Codex is now part of ChapterPal's business logic.

A significant part of what I do when I work on ChapterPal is to run a conversion from LaTeX into Markdown and see what failed. LaTeX is very old and versatile, so some scientists use very crazy tweaks in their LaTeX sources to achieve a desired visual effect in the output PDF which fail at conversion in ChapterPal.

Previously, I was in the loop:

1. Me: run a conversion

2. Me: validate visually

3. Me: instruct a coding agent to fix the issues

4. Coding agent: makes fixes, run tests

5. Me: Go to 1.

Now me is out of the loop and all happens within a cloud run. I can only observe the process in the admin's UI.

So, the conversion code self-writes while I sleep and the conversion almost never fails because a failed conversion is analyzed by Codex and the conversion code is fixed.

items.content: They are even a bigger change when developers outright refuse to add an option that you request but they don't want to implement "for your own good." That just happened to me recently. It was for a relatively simple app, so I decided to rebuild the whole app from scratch instead. As I had mentioned in one of my earlier posts, it was not exactly a one-shot effort, but it eventually worked and I have a much better version of that app now that does exactly what I want it to do!

There's a pattern in AI: models ace the exam, then fail in the lab. 

In 2022, models that got As on...
items.content:
There's a pattern in AI: models ace the exam, then fail in the lab. 

In 2022, models that got As on multiple-choice science tests couldn't perform over 90% of those experiments in a virtual environment. 

Knowing what a boiling point is and measuring one aren't the same thing.

items.title:
My agent already forgot everything we did last week.

That sucks.

This article discusses a shared m...
items.content:
My agent already forgot everything we did last week.

That sucks.

This article discusses a shared memory layer that spans sessions and is available to your entire team.

Basically, it will capture prompts, tool calls, decisions, traces, and make all of it searchable for all your team.

We need infrastructure like this across the board now.

items.content_html:
My agent already forgot everything we did last week.

That sucks.

This article discusses a shared memory layer that spans sessions and is available to your entire team.

Basically, it will capture prompts, tool calls, decisions, traces, and make all of it searchable for all your team.

We need infrastructure like this across the board now.

items.title:
Here are 7 tips to build better AI agents.

The first agent I built:

1. Took information from a vec...
items.content:
Here are 7 tips to build better AI agents.

The first agent I built:

1. Took information from a vector store
2. Formatted it as HTML
3. Emailed it to the user

It didn't get simpler than that, and yet, this agent failed about 1% of the time.

No warning or errors. It just emailed garbage.

Here is the harsh truth:

Agents fail a lot, and they fail silently. You just can't trust an LLM to do the right thing every time.

I've already built and deployed a couple of dozen agents, and here are some of the things that actually work:

1. Every agent should produce traces showing the full request flow, model interactions, token usage, and timing metadata.

2. Guardrails on inputs and outputs. Everything that goes into and comes out of an LLM should be checked by deterministic code.

3. LLM-as-a-judge evaluations. Build a simple judge using an LLM to evaluate your agent's outputs automatically.

4. Error analysis. You can collect failure samples, categorize them, and diagnose the most frequent mistakes.

5. Context engineering. Often, agents fail because their context is noisy, overloaded, or irrelevant. Learning how to keep context relevant is huge.

6. Never connect directly to a model. Instead, set up a gateway to implement observability, caching, cost management, and routing.

7. Human feedback loops. Sometimes the best guardrail is a human in the loop, especially for high-stakes decisions.

items.title: Most work on video object removal treats the problem as inpainting: fill in the pixels where the obj...
items.content:
Most work on video object removal treats the problem as inpainting: fill in the pixels where the object used to be.

That framing ignores the fact that objects in real scenes do things—they hold up other objects, cast shadows, block what would otherwise move. Remove the hand and the ball it was holding should fall.

VOID is an attempt to train a video diffusion model to handle this counterfactual reasoning, using paired training data where one video contains an object and the other shows what the scene would have looked like without it.

The paired data comes from two sources with known physics: a rigid-body simulator and a motion-capture dataset of people interacting with objects, which together give around 6,400 examples.

The authors show how far a diffusion model can be pushed toward physical plausibility when the training signal explicitly encodes cause and effect rather than just appearance.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/p6PvNdk2jg

PDF: https://t.co/ZgdTHYnkst

items.summary: Burkov discusses VOID, a research paper on video object removal that uses counterfactual physical reasoning rather than simple inpainting, trained on paired data from rigid-body simulators and motion-capture.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical content about a novel approach to video editing with physical reasoning. Well-explained by a credible ML author.

items.content_html:
Most work on video object removal treats the problem as inpainting: fill in the pixels where the object used to be.

That framing ignores the fact that objects in real scenes do things—they hold up other objects, cast shadows, block what would otherwise move. Remove the hand and the ball it was holding should fall.

VOID is an attempt to train a video diffusion model to handle this counterfactual reasoning, using paired training data where one video contains an object and the other shows what the scene would have looked like without it.

The paired data comes from two sources with known physics: a rigid-body simulator and a motion-capture dataset of people interacting with objects, which together give around 6,400 examples.

The authors show how far a diffusion model can be pushed toward physical plausibility when the training signal explicitly encodes cause and effect rather than just appearance.

items.title:
The Los Angeles Rams' GM just name-dropped Anthropic to a room full of NFL executives.

Les Snead to...
items.content:
The Los Angeles Rams' GM just name-dropped Anthropic to a room full of NFL executives.

Les Snead told the league's annual meeting in Phoenix: "We turned it all over to Claude."

It was a joke, but NFL teams are actually feeding game film into computer vision models to evaluate draft prospects.

Snead calls Claude his "assistant lieutenant", an evaluator that won't suck up to the boss (debatable). 

The problem it's solving: college players don't wear tracking sensors, so teams have zero in-game speed or movement data on draft prospects. 

Companies like Teamworks and SkillCorner use computer vision to pull those metrics directly from game film. 

That includes scanning tape from small schools most teams never send a human scout to visit.

In addition to the vision upgrades, teams now have a Copilot-powered platform to query years of combine results in plain English. 

Hawk-Eye cameras, similar to MLB's ABS system, are now in every NFL stadium, and the league is about to start distributing that video for analysis.

The analytics revolution took decades to change how teams build rosters. The AI-driven one is moving a lot faster.

items.title:
They won't come at it in how you expect

ChatGPT etc isn't going to launch a 1) Remote OK or 2) Phot...
items.content:
They won't come at it in how you expect

ChatGPT etc isn't going to launch a 1) Remote OK or 2) Photo AI or 3) MAKE book

But they have and will make it easy in the chat interface to 1) find a remote job, 2) take AI photos and 3) learn how to build a startup

I'm not a Luddite so I'm not against it, I'm just observing it, and I knew this would happen already from 2022 when the new AI wave started

My defenses are audience and community and trying to own stock in all of BigTech and BigAI

items.title:
My view is simple:

The winners in enterprise AI will not be the companies with access to the most m...
items.content:
My view is simple:

The winners in enterprise AI will not be the companies with access to the most models.

They will be the ones that build the best intelligent systems around them.

If you want the full breakdown, watch the video and tell me what part of your AI stack you are rethinking first.

What do you think becomes the real source of advantage in the next 12 months, the model itself, or the orchestration layer around it?

Check out the full article: https://t.co/XvcQeN6FDy

items.title:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but beca...
items.content:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but because they are still thinking at the model layer.

The real shift is happening one level up, where AI becomes a system that can reason, use tools, retain context, and execute work inside secure environments.

That changes enterprise strategy faster than most leaders realize.

A thread, with insights from my latest video with NVIDIA…

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC

items.title: @MorePerfectUS I've been interviewing Waymo customers. They all say it is a way better way to get ar...
items.content:
@MorePerfectUS I've been interviewing Waymo customers. They all say it is a way better way to get around a city than taking a vehicle driven by a human. 

Women, in particular, feel far safer in them because in Uber's they often get harrassed, sometimes raped. 

And then we have to talk about the safety. Human driven cars cause far more accidents and death.

So you are voting to prop up a system that isn't as good and isn't as safe just to protect jobs. 

Not to mention that labor could be used to do something much more productive for all of us. Which means you are dooming your economy to being less efficient than, say, China, which is far more aggressive about moving into autonomy.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer defends Waymo, citing customer interviews showing women feel safer, fewer accidents than human drivers, and argues against protecting jobs at expense of efficiency, noting China's more aggressive autonomy adoption.
items.reasoning: Substantive argument about autonomous vehicles with safety and economic competitiveness angles. The China comparison is relevant to AI policy discussions. Decent engagement (182 likes).

@MorePerfectUS I've been interviewing Waymo customers. They all say it is a way better way to get around a city than taking a vehicle driven by a human.

Women, in particular, feel far safer in them because in Uber's they often get harrassed, sometimes raped.

And then we have to talk about the safety. Human driven cars cause far more accidents and death.

So you are voting to prop up a system that isn't as good and isn't as safe just to protect jobs.

Not to mention that labor could be used to do something much more productive for all of us. Which means you are dooming your economy to being less efficient than, say, China, which is far more aggressive about moving into autonomy.

items.title:
My biggest takeaway:

Models are becoming components, not products.

What matters now is the system ...
items.content:
My biggest takeaway:

Models are becoming components, not products.

What matters now is the system around them:

→ runtimes
→ memory
→ tool access
→ orchestration
→ secure execution environments

That is what turns a model into an agent, and an agent into something the business can actually deploy across functions.

items.title: Cards are prepackaged workflows/prompts that you can run over and over again. Pretty similar idea to...
items.content:
Cards are prepackaged workflows/prompts that you can run over and over again. Pretty similar idea to Skills.

They have a whole library full of cards, but you can create your own.

For example, you can have a card that does all of this:

• Given a topic, it finds related conversations in Slack
• It also finds them in Gmail
• Pulls together all of that information
• Augments it with a Google Search
• Sends an email with the results

Pretty awesome stuff!

The Do agent is also pretty cool: You ask the browser to do something, and it does it.

For example, "Book me a table at Restaurant X for this date and time, add it to my calendar, and send a Slack message to my assistant with the details."

Here is the link to try this out: https://t.co/kpTqWAiQyX

Thanks to the team for partnering with me on this post.

Cards are prepackaged workflows/prompts that you can run over and over again. Pretty similar idea to Skills.

They have a whole library full of cards, but you can create your own.

For example, you can have a card that does all of this:

• Given a topic, it finds related conversations in Slack

• It also finds them in Gmail

• Pulls together all of that information

• Augments it with a Google Search

• Sends an email with the results

Pretty awesome stuff!

The Do agent is also pretty cool: You ask the browser to do something, and it does it.

For example, "Book me a table at Restaurant X for this date and time, add it to my calendar, and send a Slack message to my assistant with the details."

Here is the link to try this out: https://t.co/kpTqWAiQyX

Thanks to the team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title:
RAG is not broken, naive vector-only RAG fails at scale.

What’s broken is the idea that meaning = v...
items.content:
RAG is not broken, naive vector-only RAG fails at scale.

What’s broken is the idea that meaning = vector similarity.

At scale, that collapses.

The future is neurosymbolic AI:
•ontologies for structure
•deterministic layers for truth
•LLMs for interpretation (not computation)

items.summary_html: DeepLearn007 argues RAG isn't broken but naive vector-only RAG fails at scale. Advocates neurosymbolic AI combining ontologies, deterministic layers, and LLMs for interpretation to eliminate retrieval-driven hallucinations.
items.content_html:
RAG is not broken, naive vector-only RAG fails at scale.

What’s broken is the idea that meaning = vector similarity.

At scale, that collapses.

The future is neurosymbolic AI:

•ontologies for structure

•deterministic layers for truth

•LLMs for interpretation (not computation)

That’s how you eliminate retrieval-driven hallucinations. @pierrepinna @ipfconline1

items.title:
The second misconception I keep seeing:

Too many teams think they have to choose between open model...
items.content:
The second misconception I keep seeing:

Too many teams think they have to choose between open models and proprietary models.

They do not.

The smarter path is hybrid.

→ proprietary models for scale and broad capability
→ open models for flexibility and control
→ orchestration layers to route each task to the best-fit model in real time

That is how you balance performance, cost, and governance without locking yourself into one lane.

items.title: @navtechai If such living, self‑organizing nervous systems are integrated with neuromorphic spiking ...
items.content:
@navtechai If such living, self‑organizing nervous systems are integrated with neuromorphic spiking hardware and neurosymbolic cognitive architectures, they form a plausible long‑term pathway toward AGI.

Neuromorphic platforms (Loihi, TrueNorth, SpiNNaker, BrainScaleS, etc.) aim for spiking, event‑driven, co‑located memory/compute with online learning and low power—closer to brain dynamics than GPU LLMs.

Some AGI roadmaps explicitly frame neuromorphic computing as a candidate route to real‑time, lifelong‑learning AGI/post‑AGI ecosystems.

Current surveys argue that integrating neural perception/learning with symbolic structure/reasoning is one of the most promising ways to get robust generalization, compositionality, and meta‑cognition.

items.title:
@ben_golub That's a bit too harsh.  Here's ChatGPT's summary:

Probably not a misunderstanding in th...
items.content:
@ben_golub That's a bit too harsh.  Here's ChatGPT's summary:

Probably not a misunderstanding in the strict sense. It looks more like Pinker is using a psychological, operationalized version of common knowledge rather than insisting on the fully formal game-theoretic one.

In the formal literature, common knowledge is the infinite hierarchy: not just “A knows p and B knows p,” but “A knows that B knows p, B knows that A knows p,” and so on indefinitely. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes both that this infinite hierarchy is the classical definition and that philosophers and game theorists have long worried about whether humans literally represent it in their heads.

Pinker seems aware of that. In a 2025 interview, he explicitly says that common knowledge “in the technical sense” involves an infinite number of embedded beliefs, and then says humans do not consciously run through all those loops. Instead, he argues that public, salient events like eye contact can generate what he calls “implicit common knowledge” in one stroke.

So my judgment is:

If your standard is strict Aumann-style formalism, Pinker is using the term more loosely than the cleanest technical definition.
If your standard is whether he grasped the formal concept, the available evidence suggests yes, he did. He seems to know the formal definition and is intentionally translating it into a psychologically plausible account.

A fair criticism would be that this move can blur an important distinction between:

genuine formal common knowledge,
public signals that are merely very strong coordination devices, and
weaker notions like common belief or “close enough for practice.”

That distinction matters in some game-theoretic settings, because near-common-knowledge and full common knowledge can behave differently. The SEP discusses exactly this issue and notes various weakenings such as probabilistic or approximate common knowledge.

So the strongest answer is: not a basic misunderstanding, but arguably a deliberate softening of the concept that a formalist could think is too loose.

items.title:
Opera is now packed with a bunch of good ideas.

Their @opera_neon_ browser comes with several agent...
items.content:
Opera is now packed with a bunch of good ideas.

Their @opera_neon_ browser comes with several agents that will make your relationship with a browser very different:

• You can ask the browser to complete tasks
• You can make things (images, videos, web apps...)
• You can chat with it
• You can do research

The two best features: the Do agent and the idea of Cards.

I now want these everywhere!

items.title:
✨ Progress on my drone sim 🚁 Drone of War:

https://t.co/q1vYLwMhcW

Removed my old giant city FBX, ...
items.content:
✨ Progress on my drone sim 🚁 Drone of War:

https://t.co/q1vYLwMhcW

Removed my old giant city FBX, because 1) it wasn't made by AI, 2) it was way too big

Made the scenery a sandy war land now and just generating my own 3d assets now with @cursor_ai and @tripoai  (sponsors of #vibejam) 

Also removed the drone sound, I thought a drone operator probably doesn't hear the drone sound anyway (only if it's near them) and I was inspired by @denisbondare to add some sine wave sounds and it made some buddhist type dings that I really like

The paradox of war + buddhist sounds is kinda interesting combo, there's something disturbing about drone warfare where it's just people remotely controlling it I wanted to capture that in a game

Also added real soldiers models (also generated), they're static though, but they do move, but I need to make them move somehow, not sure how yet, soldiers shoot at you and try take you down

Also need to add tanks and other military vehicles that drive around

And then I need to add more of a gameplay element, I still think about multiplayer where you choose to be either a drone or a soldier/tank/etc so it's drone vs everyone else!

P.S. I can't participate in the #vibejam as the organizer of course but I still like to make some fun games just for fun and to show the tools!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio shares progress on his drone simulator game 'Drone of War' built with AI tools (Cursor, Tripo AI). Uses AI-generated 3D assets, explores paradox of war + buddhist sounds, considering multiplayer with drone vs soldiers/tanks gameplay.
items.reasoning: Interesting showcase of AI-assisted game development using multiple AI tools. 231 likes, 52K views. Demonstrates practical vibe coding.
items.summary_html: Levelsio shares progress on his drone simulator game 'Drone of War' built with AI tools (Cursor, Tripo AI). Uses AI-generated 3D assets, explores paradox of war + buddhist sounds, considering multiplayer with drone vs soldiers/tanks gameplay.
items.content_html:
✨ Progress on my drone sim 🚁 Drone of War:

https://t.co/q1vYLwMhcW

Removed my old giant city FBX, because 1) it wasn't made by AI, 2) it was way too big

Made the scenery a sandy war land now and just generating my own 3d assets now with @cursor_ai and @tripoai (sponsors of #vibejam)

Also removed the drone sound, I thought a drone operator probably doesn't hear the drone sound anyway (only if it's near them) and I was inspired by @denisbondare to add some sine wave sounds and it made some buddhist type dings that I really like

The paradox of war + buddhist sounds is kinda interesting combo, there's something disturbing about drone warfare where it's just people remotely controlling it I wanted to capture that in a game

Also added real soldiers models (also generated), they're static though, but they do move, but I need to make them move somehow, not sure how yet, soldiers shoot at you and try take you down

Also need to add tanks and other military vehicles that drive around

And then I need to add more of a gameplay element, I still think about multiplayer where you choose to be either a drone or a soldier/tank/etc so it's drone vs everyone else!

P.S. I can't participate in the #vibejam as the organizer of course but I still like to make some fun games just for fun and to show the tools!

items.title: IMHO, one of the roots of all evil in ANY organization is a fixed, entrenched, and immutable hierarc...
items.content: IMHO, one of the roots of all evil in ANY organization is a fixed, entrenched, and immutable hierarchy. Doesn’t matter if you have 10, 5, 2, or 0 layers in the org chart. This particularly pernicious in tech where technology and business circumstances change extremely rapidly, especially now in the age of AI. Ideally there ought to be a mechanism for an org chart to evolve dynamically as circumstances change. https://t.co/3D5q5dWZEU

items.title:
Indexes aren’t the problem.

Unbounded indexing in high-ingestion systems is.

IoT, observability, A...
items.content:
Indexes aren’t the problem.

Unbounded indexing in high-ingestion systems is.

IoT, observability, AI telemetry, financial feeds all share the same pattern:

→ continuous ingestion
→ append-only data
→ time-based queries
→ massive retention

Eventually the architecture matters more than tuning.

Tiger Data published the full breakdown here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K 

Check out the full article: https://t.co/ERSbjhAOKu
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.reasoning: Sponsored database infrastructure content. Tangentially relevant to AI infrastructure but primarily a partnership promotion.

items.content_html:
Indexes aren’t the problem.

Unbounded indexing in high-ingestion systems is.

IoT, observability, AI telemetry, financial feeds all share the same pattern:

→ continuous ingestion

→ append-only data

→ time-based queries

→ massive retention

Eventually the architecture matters more than tuning.

items.title:
Most database slowdowns don’t start with an outage.

They start with a “fix.”

You add an index.
Que...
items.content:
Most database slowdowns don’t start with an outage.

They start with a “fix.”

You add an index.
Queries get faster.
Dashboards recover.

Everyone moves on.

But at scale, every new index quietly multiplies your write load.

This is the index trap I discussed with @TigerDatabase.

Tiger Data's deep dive on the optimization treadmill covers the full picture: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K 

#TigerDataPartner
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.reasoning: Clearly marked partner content. Some technical substance about database scaling.

items.title:
Now layer in database mechanics.

→ WAL logs every heap + index change
→ B-tree indexes must be trav...
items.content:
Now layer in database mechanics.

→ WAL logs every heap + index change
→ B-tree indexes must be traversed for each insert
→ Time-series data hits the rightmost index pages
→ Autovacuum activity increases as tables grow

The result is a predictable treadmill.

Add index → queries faster
Write load increases → latency rises
Scale infra → repeat

Tiger Data’s deep dive on this pattern: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.title:
Indexes accelerate reads.

They also multiply writes.

Simple math:

1 insert →

→ 1 heap write
→ +1...
items.content:
Indexes accelerate reads.

They also multiply writes.

Simple math:

1 insert →

→ 1 heap write
→ +1 write per index

So if you have:

→ 5 indexes → 6 writes per insert

At scale:

50k inserts/sec → 300k writes/sec
100k inserts/sec → 600k writes/sec

And that’s before maintenance.

Tiger Data explains the full impact here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.summary: Part of Tiger Data thread on how indexes multiply writes at scale.
items.reasoning: Sponsored technical content, minimal engagement.

items.title:
When queries slow down, the instinct is simple.

Add indexes.

→ Composite indexes on device_id + ti...
items.content:
When queries slow down, the instinct is simple.

Add indexes.

→ Composite indexes on device_id + timestamp
→ Partial indexes for recent data
→ Covering indexes for hot queries

Result → queries drop back to milliseconds.

But something important just changed.

Every insert now got more expensive.

Tiger Data breaks this down in detail here: https://t.co/kpbi4eyG9K

items.content:
The old workflow:

→ Build deck
→ Schedule meeting
→ Repeat the same presentation 20 times
→ Hope people remember it

The new workflow:

→ Upload your PowerPoint
→ Keep your layout, fonts, branding
→ Speaker notes become AI voiceover
→ Instant video scenes, not screen recordings

No camera. No re-recording. No production team.

I have Persian heritage and support freedom for the people.

There is a meaningful Iranian‑related (Iran Neolithic) strand in the deep ancestry of many European populations.
